Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty scattered because I don't have internet at home, so I had to plan everything around when I could get to the library or campus computer labs. I'd spend long stretches there when I could, sometimes four or five hours at a time, downloading everything I might need for the week. It wasn't ideal, but I made it work by being really intentional about what I grabbed during those sessions. I kept a notebook where I'd write down what I needed to review offline later.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot based on when I could actually get access to computers with internet. Some weeks I'd be at the library three times, other weeks maybe just once if I had work or other things going on. Deadlines definitely pushed me to find ways to get online more frequently, even if it meant staying late after the library technically closed. I tried to create some regularity, but life kept throwing curveballs at me.
Q3: Usually I logged in because I needed to download materials for the week or check if there were any announcements I'd missed. Sometimes I'd get anxious about falling behind, and that would push me to find a way to get online even when it was inconvenient. I also logged in to participate in discussions when I could, since I knew that interaction was important even though I couldn't do it as often as other students.
Q4: It became routine in the sense that every time I got to campus, the first thing I'd do was log in and check everything. It was like a ritual – get to the library, find a computer, download what I need, check discussions, maybe post something. The routine formed out of necessity rather than convenience. I had to be strategic about my limited online time, so it became automatic to maximize every session.
Q5: The video lectures were honestly lifesavers because I could download them and watch them over and over on my phone offline. I'd watch them multiple times, pausing and rewinding, really trying to absorb everything since I couldn't just pop back online to rewatch. The discussion boards were also helpful because seeing how other students understood concepts gave me different perspectives. I'm a hands-on learner, so anything I could practice with was valuable.
Q6: I often skipped the supplementary readings that were only available online because I couldn't always download them in formats that worked offline. Some of the interactive quizzes I couldn't use much either since they required constant internet connection. It wasn't that I didn't want to use them – I just literally couldn't access them when I was doing most of my studying at home without internet.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see how it connected to what I'd be tested on or when it helped something finally click in my brain. I'm kinesthetic, so anything where I could practice or apply concepts felt more valuable than just reading. If I could take it offline with me, that automatically made it more worthwhile because I knew I'd actually be able to use it.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I often couldn't afford to pursue, honestly. With my limited access and time, I had to prioritize required stuff first. But sometimes the optional things looked really interesting, and I'd feel a bit sad that I couldn't explore them more. Required activities had this weight to them that made me push harder to complete them no matter what.
Q9: Oh absolutely, most of my studying happened offline. I'd download PDFs, save videos to my phone, take handwritten notes from everything, and then study from those at home. I also borrowed textbooks from the library and would photocopy sections I needed. My notebook became like my own personal textbook with everything rewritten in ways that made sense to me.
Q10: There were many nights where I'd be at my kitchen table for hours working through problems by hand, reviewing my notes, watching downloaded videos repeatedly. None of that showed up anywhere in the LMS. I'd also study with a classmate in person sometimes, quizzing each other and working through concepts together. That peer interaction happened face-to-face, not through the system.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd access a video or document once online, download it immediately, and then spend hours with it offline over the following days. One login might represent a week's worth of actual studying for me. I became really good at knowing exactly what I needed to grab during my limited online windows.
Q12: There were definitely periods where everything felt overwhelming, especially when I was dealing with some personal sadness and the stress of not having reliable internet. Sometimes I'd feel so down that even getting to the library felt like climbing a mountain. Financial stress also made it hard to focus because I'd be worrying about other things instead of the course material.
Q13: Fatigue was a big one – I'd sometimes be so tired from working and commuting that when I finally got online, I couldn't absorb anything properly. Competing responsibilities constantly affected my LMS use because I had to prioritize survival stuff sometimes over schoolwork. The workload felt manageable when I could focus, but confusion would pile up when I couldn't ask questions in real-time.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd be logged in, scrolling through materials, but my mind was somewhere else entirely. I'd be thinking about bills or family stuff or just feeling this heavy sadness that made it hard to concentrate. I'd realize I'd been staring at the same page for twenty minutes without actually reading it. Those sessions felt wasted even though they showed up as engagement.
Q15: I definitely postponed assignments even when I knew they were important, usually because I felt overwhelmed or didn't have the energy to do them justice. Sometimes the sadness just made it hard to start anything. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I felt better, but tomorrow would come with its own challenges. The low assignment completion rate reflects that struggle.
Q16: Deadlines were like emergency alarms that would force me into action mode. I'd find ways to get online no matter what when something was due, even if it meant missing other things. They created this intense pressure that could be motivating but also stressful. I learned to work backwards from deadlines to plan my limited online access around them.
Q17: When I got feedback, it actually motivated me to try harder next time, even if the feedback wasn't great. Knowing someone was paying attention to my work made me feel less invisible. But sometimes feedback came when I couldn't access it immediately, so by the time I saw it, the moment had passed a bit. Positive feedback especially helped lift my mood during harder times.
Q18: The structure helped in some ways because I knew what was expected and when. But the assumption that everyone has constant internet access made some parts harder for me. I appreciated when materials were downloadable and when deadlines had some flexibility. The discussion requirements were challenging since I couldn't participate as spontaneously as others.
Q19: Downloadable content made engagement much easier for me – that was crucial. What made it harder was anything that required real-time participation or constant connectivity. The peer interaction components were tough because I couldn't respond quickly to discussions. I wished there were more offline-friendly options for someone in my situation.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me who's downloading everything they can during their online time but then doing the actual work offline where it doesn't get tracked. Or maybe they're struggling with actually completing things due to other life factors, even though they're trying hard. High activity might show effort and intention even when output is low.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience somewhat. I performed reasonably well on exams because I studied hard offline, but my LMS activity was limited by my access constraints. Someone might look at my login frequency and think I wasn't engaged, but I was actually putting in serious hours with downloaded materials. The system just couldn't see where my real learning was happening.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of access and what resources people click on, which gives some indication of engagement. But they completely miss offline studying, the quality of attention during sessions, emotional state while engaging, and learning that happens through non-digital means. They can't capture someone reading printed notes at 2am or discussing concepts with a friend over coffee.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions. It's when I'm actively thinking, questioning, connecting ideas to other things I know. For me, engagement often looks like sitting with my notebook, working through problems until they make sense, even when I'm tired or sad. It's a mental commitment that doesn't require being online.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how hard I fought to stay in this course despite not having internet at home and dealing with financial stress and periods of real sadness. It wouldn't show the hours I spent at the library or the notebooks full of handwritten notes. It can't capture my resilience or how much this degree means to me, even when everything felt difficult. My engagement was deep, just not always visible.
